2 min read
1 hour ago

--

“Every learning journey begins with a question. Mine began with many.”
As a QA Engineer with over seven years of experience, I’ve spent most of my career ensuring software quality through manual testing, API testing, SQL validation, regression testing, and Agile practices.
As I progressed in my career, I noticed a common trend across job descriptions, technical discussions, and career roadmaps — automation testing had become an essential skill for modern QA engineers.
That realization sparked a series of questions in my mind.

• Why should I move from manual testing to automation?

• Which programming language should I learn?

• Why does everyone recommend JavaScript?

• Is JavaScript really worth learning for a QA career?

• Where should I begin?
If you’re a Manual QA Engineer exploring automation, you’ve probably asked yourself some of these same questions.
I certainly did.
For a long time, I wasn’t looking for the “best” programming language — I was looking for the right starting point. With so many options available, choosing where to invest my time wasn’t easy.
Choosing the Right Programming Language
The technology landscape offers several excellent programming languages for automation, including Java, Python, C#, JavaScript, and others. Each has its own strengths, community, and learning curve.
Rather than choosing one based on popularity or recommendations alone, I wanted to understand what modern QA teams were actually using in real-world projects.
So, I started researching.
I explored job postings, read technical blogs, watched conference talks, browsed GitHub repositories, and looked into the automation frameworks being adopted across the industry.
One thing quickly became clear.
JavaScript was everywhere.
That was the moment my JavaScript learning journey truly began.
Why I’m Writing This Series
There’s a saying that one of the best ways to learn is to teach.
This series is my way of documenting everything I learn as I transition from manual testing to automation using JavaScript and Playwright.
My goal isn’t to write a textbook or simply explain syntax. Instead, I want to create practical, easy-to-understand notes that connect JavaScript concepts with real automation testing scenarios.
As I learn each topic, I’ll share:

• Simple explanations without unnecessary jargon.

• Practical code examples.

• Key concepts that every QA Engineer should know.

• Interview-focused insights.

• How each JavaScript concept applies to Playwright automation.
I’m writing these articles not only to reinforce my own learning but also to help other QA professionals who are beginning the same journey.
If you’re starting your transition from manual testing to automation, I hope this series becomes a helpful companion — one that makes JavaScript easier to understand and less intimidating.
This is just the beginning of my journey, and I’m excited to learn, document, and grow one concept at a time.
**See you in the next article, where I’ll share why I chose JavaScript
